Nickel(II)-modified chitosan hydrogel: A hybrid sorbent for removal of 
phosphate anions from water

Łukasz Wujcicki a, Tomasz Ma´ndok b, Dominika Paprota b, Krzysztof Piotrowski c, 
Jakub Adamek d, Maciej Krzywiecki e, Karolina Goc f, Joanna Kluczka a,*

A R T I C L E I N F O

A B S T R A C T

Keywords:
Chitosan
Phosphate anions removal
Sorption

A novel nickel-modified chitosan sorbent (Cs–Ni), highly efficient in phosphates removal from aqueous solu-
tions, was proposed. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and point of 
zero charges of sorbent were employed to determine its structure and properties. Batch sorption experiments 
allowed to identify the Cs–Ni high phosphate sorption capacity, exceeding 62.5 mg/g (with the removal effi-
ciency of 73 % - for optimal experimental conditions: pH 7, sorbent dose 10 g/dm3, temperature 293 K, contact 
time 1440 min). The equilibrium data for the new Cs–Ni sorbent and aqueous solution of phosphates fitted well 
with the Freundlich, Redlich-Peterson, Hill, Sips, Toth, and Khan sorption isotherms (R2 = 0.988–0.992), sug-
gesting sorbate distributions other than a creation of monolayer on the heterogeneous sorption surface. The 
phosphates sorption on the new sorbent resulted from the electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged 
(cid:0)
anions and the positively charged Cs–Ni surface, formation of stable complexes between these 
H2PO4
anions and nickel(II) species, and the anions direct sorption by interacting with the free and active chitosan 
+
OPO(OH)2). The results highlighted the promising potential

of the Cs–Ni hydrogel for phosphate anions removal from contaminated water.

1. Introduction

Surface and groundwater pollution is a significant, common, and 
complex environmental problem, which is becoming more and more 
important nowadays [1]. One of the key pollutants that deteriorate 
water quality is phosphorus compounds, originating from both the 
natural phenomena of biomass decomposition and the wastewater from 
various technological processes or agricultural fertilization. The most 
prevalent forms of phosphorus in wastewater are phosphate anions [2]. 
High concentration of phosphates (PO₄3(cid:0)
) in the surface water is closely 

related to eutrophication - a phenomenon unfavorable for the biosphere, 
resulting in excessive aquatic vegetation and consequently, its slow 
biodegradation, which negatively affects the biodiversity and strongly 
disturbs the stability of aqueous ecosystems [3].

The primary natural sources of phosphorus are phosphate rocks, 
which are considered to be a non-renewable resource [4]. They are 
however intensively exploited for a production of phosphoric and 
superphosphoric acids – main market substrates necessary for mineral 
fertilizers, and animal feed supplements manufacture [5]. This common 
in agricultural 
use of phosphorus supplements and 

fertilizers

However, from another point of view, the same wastewater is starting to 
be seen as a secondary, and in the future perhaps the only available, 
source of phosphorus. For this reason, it is necessary to identify and 
continually technologically improve effective methods for separation 
and recycling of phosphates from the aquatic environment, taking into 
account in particular the problem of dilution of phosphorus compounds, 
co-existence of their different chemical forms, as well as co-presence of 
other chemical species in complex physicochemical systems of waste-
water, derived from different sources [9] (Fig. 1).

One promising approach for not only efficient, but also possibly se-
lective separation of phosphate pollutants is based on using various 
sorbents [10–13]. In recent years, some new hydrogel-based materials 
are the subject of research due to the possibility of a practical use of a 
number of their new separation capabilities, resulting directly from their 
specific chemical structure, and the advantageous physical properties in 
the field of controlled separation mechanisms. The following properties 
of these systems are particularly useful: developed large sorption sur-
face, ability to swell in water, and possibilities of directed “corrections” 
of surface chemistry [14–16]. Among the hydrogels based on natural 
compounds, chitosan, a polysaccharide derived from chitin, is the sub-
ject of intense laboratory tests [17–19].

Chitosan demonstrates many unique and advantageous properties, 
like biocompatibility, biodegradability, and low toxicity. In particular, a 
possibility of a formation of surface complexes with various specially 
selected anions and cations can lead to unique microstructural concepts 
and synergistic solutions in terms of increased possibilities of effective 
and selective sorption of different compounds. This makes it a very 
convenient and modifiable matrix for tailored sorption materials for-
mation [20,21]. Chitosan hydrogels, doped on the surface with appro-
priate active ingredients, can be used with a high flexibility, playing the 
role of composite materials with catalytic, adsorption or absorption 
abilities, designed towards certain pollutants removal [22,23]. The use 
of such modified chitosan to eliminate phosphate anions from 

wastewater can enable much more efficient separation of phosphorus 
compounds and their subsequent technological recovery. This includes, 
however, optimal designing of composite materials, while considering 
not only identified chemical affinity, but also adequate spatial disper-
sion of active centres, closely related to large enough specific surface 
area of the interphase contact.

Chemical modifications of chitosan matrix, such as the dispersion of 
chemically active metal forms in the chitosan skeleton, can significantly 
enhance its sorption capacity towards phosphates, due to the increase in 
the number of co-acting separating mechanisms [24–27]. An under-
standing of the sorption mechanisms, their interactions, and suscepti-
bility to the influence of various factors controlling the reaction can 
make the optimization of phosphorus removal processes [28,29]. Met-
al–chitosan composites are regarded to be materials relatively simple to 
manufacture [30]. Introduction of metals, such as lanthanum [31], 
zirconium [32], iron [33], magnesium [34], and calcium [35] into the 
chitosan matrix was reported in the literature. Ni2+
ions dispersed 
within the chitosan hydrogel matrix can potentially form numerous 
sparingly soluble compounds with (PO4)3(cid:0)
or many stable complexes, 
also with other species of phosphorus present in the wastewater, like

On the other hand, some papers are available that use phosphorus 
compounds to bind Ni2+
from aqueous solutions. The authors’ chemical 
concept was thus on the reverse binding mechanism. Examples include 
works on nickel sorption on different phosphorus forms, like titanium 
phosphate [37], focusing on high capacity for nickel binding, which is 
indirectly related to the strong interactions during the formation of a 
stable, sparingly soluble nickel phosphate Ni3(PO4)2 (pKsp = 30.3) [36]. 
The stable interactions formed between the Ni2+
cation and the 
(P3O10)5(cid:0)
anion were used in a subsequent study [38], where nickel ions 
adsorbed on chitosan cross-linked with tripolyphosphate.

In this work, an original chemical concept of integrating nickel with 
chitosan hydrogel matrix to develop a novel sorbent for the separation of 
phosphorus from an aqueous solution was experimentally verified in 
batch and dynamic systems. This research covered both sorption kinetics

and equilibrium (isotherms), along with a comprehensive analysis of a 
phosphate’s sorption mechanism. A hypothesis in the study was that 
nickel ions could actively participate in binding phosphate ions from the 
solution. A potential synergistic effect between chitosan in its hydrogel 
form and Ni metal ions within its structure was assumed. The chitosan 
hydrogel should, due to its swelling capacity, especially facilitate spatial 
nickel dispersion and advantageously increase the exposition of Ni- 
based active sites within a flexibly spongy structure, thus improving 
the phosphorous binding efficiency. Similar solution, where nickel was 
added to the chitosan matrix, was used for efficient removal of borates 
from an aqueous solution [39]. Chemical conditions, or to be more 
precise phosphate affinity, directed authors’ attention to nickel ions, 
which were not previously used in the system for phosphate sorption.

2. Experimental section

2.1. Chemicals and instruments

2.1.1. Chemicals

All reagents were used without additional purification and in their 
commercially available forms. Deionized water: supported by Millipore 
Elix 10 system with a conductivity of 0.067 μS/cm was used. Potassium 
dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (CAS 
number: 7778-77-0). Standard solutions of phosphates (1000 mg/dm3 
P-PO4, code number: 104690) and nickel (1000 mg/dm3 Ni, code 
number: 119792) were provided by Merck. Chitosan, HMW, high 
viscous, with a molecular weight of 600–800 kDa (the viscosity test 
determined the molecular weight to be 717 kDa using Ubbelohde 
capillary) was used in the experiments. Its degree of deacetylation (DD) 
determined previously [40], using the H1 NMR method, was 96.58 % 
and was provided by Acros Organics (code number: 428855000). Other 
reagents used in the synthesis of the sorbent were as follows: nickel(II) 
chloride (Merck, CAS number: 7718-54-9), acetic acid (Avantor Per-
formance Materials Poland S.A., CAS number: 64–19-7), and sodium 
hydroxide (Chempur, CAS number: 1310-58-3). The analytical reagent 
grade was used as a standard for all chemical materials applied.

2.1.2. Analytical methods and instruments

Liquid phases analyses were done using the ICP-OES method on the 
following analytical lines - for P: λ = 213.618 nm, λ = 214.914 nm, λ =
177.434 nm, λ = 178.222 nm, and for Ni: λ = 216.555 nm, λ = 231.604 
nm, λ = 231.604 nm, λ = 230.299 nm. A calibration curves range of 
0.1–50 mg/dm3 was established using the Varian 710-ES spectropho-
tometer. The FTIR analysis was carried out using a Spectrum Two 
spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Waltham). The XPS measurements were 
performed in an ultra-high vacuum (UHV) multi-chamber equipped 
with PREVAC EA15 hemispherical electron energy analyzer and with a 
2D-MCP detector (Prevac Sp. z o.o.), operating at a base pressure of 
(cid:0) 9 Pa. The monochromatic X-ray source (PREVAC dual-anode XR- 
9⋅10
40B, Al-Kα excitation line with energy of 1486.60 eV) was used for 
sample excitation. The pass energy (PE) was set to 200 eV for survey 
spectra (0.8 eV scanning step) and to 100 eV for high-resolution scans of 
selected energy regions (0.05 eV scanning step). All measurements were 
based on a normal take-off angle and a curved analyzer exit slit (0.8 ×
25 mm) to achieve the highest energy resolution and to minimize 
diffraction effects. The binding energy (BE) scale was calibrated using 
Au 4f7/2 (84.0 eV) region. The raw spectral data were processed and 
automatically directly interpreted by CASA XPS® software (version 
2.3.23PR01). The integration of Gauss (30 %) and Lorenz (70 %) func-
tions was performed and combined together with the Shirley function to 
minimize background effect. For viscosity tests, an Ubbelohde (K = 0.5, 
50–500 mm2/s) capillary (CONBEST, Krak´ow, Poland) was used (0.2 M 
CH3COOH and 0.1 M CH3COONa, chitosan concentrations 0.2–1 g/dm3, 
303 K). The chitosan molecular weight was calculated according to the 
Mark–Houwink equation (k = 0.01001 cm3/g, α = 0.8349) [41].

2.2. Cs–Ni sorbent fabrication

The procedure described previously [42] was adopted for this 
research to prepare the chitosan granules modified with nickel. In a 500 
cm3 conical flask, 5 g of powdered chitosan was dissolved in 250 cm3 of 
1 % CH3COOH aqueous solution, using a magnetic stirrer for mixing. 
Subsequently, 1.1041 g of solid nickel(II) chloride was introduced, to 
achieve a nickel-to-chitosan mass ratio of 1:10, and dissolved under 
continuous stirring. The prepared mixture was then transferred drop-by- 
drop into a 10 % NaOH aqueous solution using a syringe for coagulation 
and granules formation. The formed sorbent granules were washed with 
deionized water until a neutral pH and low conductivity (< 10 μS/cm) in 
the post-processed effluents were reached.

Chemical composition of the sorbent was determined using the ICP- 
OES method. The sorbent granules were dried in atmospheric air to 
eliminate moisture and then dissolved in a concentrated HNO3. The 
mineralizate was diluted 100 times for quantitative analysis. For 
comparative qualitative and quantitative analysis, the manufactured 
materials were investigated before and after sorption. Samples for FTIR 
and XPS analyses were prepared by CHRIST Alpha 1-2LDplus freeze- 
dryer, following manual pulverization in an agate mortar. The XPS 
and FTIR analyses corresponded to sorbents with a nickel-to-polymer 
ratio of 1:2, correlating with 50 wt% of nickel fraction within the dry 
sorbent (333 mg/g).

2.3. Sorption pH and pHPZC study

The influence of solution pH in a range of 4–10 on the sorption 
process was investigated using KH2PO4 solution (10 mg/dm3 P-PO4). 
The solution pH was adjusted by an addition of 0.1 M aqueous solutions 
of HCl or NaOH. Then, the Cs–Ni granules samples, 0.5 g each, after 
draining with blotting paper, were introduced into 250 cm3 conical 
flasks. Each vessel was filled with 50 cm3 of the solution, sealed with 
ground glass stoppers and shaken at 293 K for 24 h at 180 rpm 
(CHEMLAND, SK-O330-PRO). Then, the Cs–Ni sorbent granules were 
separated using analytical filters (FILTRAK, hard filters, type 390, 84 g/ 
m2). Chemical compositions of both filtrates and initial solutions were 
identified using the ICP-OES method (each analytical test was done 
twice). Sorption capacity of the Cs–Ni sorbent (q) was calculated by Eq. 
(1):

The point of zero charge (PZC) was determined alongside with the 
effect of pH on the phosphate anions sorption (CP-105 pH-meter, 
Elmetron). The initial pH was plotted against the difference in pH 
before and after the sorption, and the PZC corresponded to the point 
where the lines intersected the initial pH axis. This pH value represented 
the pHPZC.

2.7. Moisture content study

(7) 

The moisture content within the Cs–Ni sorbent structure, required 
for calculating mass balance, was determined using a moisture analyzer 
(RADWAG MA 200.3Y). The ca. 1 g of sorbent (in the form of hydrogel 
granules) was first drained with blotting paper, followed by an auto-
matic procedure implemented in the moisture analyzer. After drying 
time (ca. 30 min) determined automatically by the analyzer (stable 
constant mass detection), the results were recorded.

3. Results and discussion

3.1. Characterization of the Cs–Ni sorbent

2.6. Dynamic sorption study

3.1.1. FTIR analysis

The dynamic phosphate anions sorption tests were carried out in a 
laboratory glass column (15 mm inner diameter, 150 mm height) packed 
with ca. 10 g of the Cs–Ni sorbent. The basic technical parameters of the 
sorption, such as bed height, bed density, as well as other more specific 
information were provided in the Appendix. A peristaltic pump 
(LAMBDA Preciflow) was used to maintain a pseudo-continuous inflow 
rate of 1 cm3/min at the top of the column, allowing then for the 
aqueous phosphate solution to flow downward through the bed voids. 
To determine the sorption breakthrough curve, effluent samples of 15 
cm3 each were systematically collected from the bottom of the column 
every 15 min using Falcon tubes placed in an automatic collector 
(Kamush, LPfrac030). All collected liquid fractions were subsequently 
analyzed using the ICP-OES method. The dynamic sorption experiments 
were performed twice to verify the repeatability of the data.

The breakthrough sorption capacity of the phosphate anions, qb

3.1.2. XPS analysis

Further insight into the characteristics of the new Ni–Cs sorbents 
was done employing X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Survey spectra 
(not shown for brevity) presented all expected energy regions (i.e. C 1 s, 
N 1 s and O 1 s for all the samples and additional Ni 2p energy region for 
Cs–Ni and Cs-Ni-P ones). For the last sample, representing Cs–Ni sor-
bent after phosphates sorption, P 2p energy region was also shown. For 
the detailed description of the bonding mechanism, and as a support for 
the detailed FTIR analysis, the particular energy regions underwent 
decomposition. The common parts for all the samples decomposed re-
gions were shown in Fig. 3. Panel 3a) presents the C 1 s region, which in 
the majority was decomposed into four main components assigned for 
C–C/C–H configuration (~284.6 eV), and C-N-O/C-NH/C-O-C con-
figurations [49]. The multiple assignment rationalized the overall 
component intensity with respect to the primary C–C one. The last two 
components should be ascribed to C––O/C-OH and C-OOH, respectively. 
However, the fourth one was visible only for Cs–Ni sample [50]. The 
last one was most likely a result of the unwanted atmosphere species

3.2. Phosphate anions sorption

3.2.1. pH effect

The initial solution pH influence on the phosphates sorption from the 
aqueous solutions using the Cs–Ni sorbent is graphically demonstrated 
in Fig. 4a. The results indicated its significant influence on the sorption 
effects. When the pH increased, the sorbent ability (quantified by its 
capacity parameter) to bind phosphates decreased. A particularly sig-
nificant effect of sorption capacity drop was experimentally identified 
for pH range ≥ 8. This phenomenon might be explained by two main

active sites. On the contrary, the PFO kinetic model is based on an 
assumption of an existence of only a few active sites within its structure. 
The compatibility of the PSO kinetic with the experimental data was also 
consistent with the independent analytical information about large 
structural development of potential sorption surface with active sites 
(both from Ni dispersion, as well as derived directly from the intrinsic 
chemical structure of chitosan surface). It was also suggested by a higher 
correlation to Freundlich isotherm, based on an assumption of a het-
erogeneous surface and multilayer sorption effects.

At a process temperature of 298 K, increasing the initial concentra-
tion of phosphates from c1 = 10 up to c2 = 50 mg/dm3 led to significant 
changes in the equilibrium concentration of the sorbate. This concen-
tration rose from approximately 7.53 mg/g to about 11.81 mg/g, which 
aligned closely with the measured data of around 7.43 and ca. 11.44 
mg/g. The values of the k2 parameter of the PSO model also indicated 
change in the process rate, especially in its first phase (k2 = 0.0015 → 
0.00056 (g/(mg • h))). Increasing the process temperature up to 308 K 
caused different changes in both q2 and k2 parameters. For the higher 
process temperature, a similar trend in the q2 parameter was observed, 
along with a five-fold change in the initial concentration of the sorbate 
(q2 ca. 7.90 → 21.15 mg/g), but this change was almost three-fold. The 
k2 parameter value for the higher temperature was almost identical to 
that at 298 K for a lower initial concentration. For a concentration of 50 
mg/dm3, a significant decrease in the k2 parameter (0.00056 → 0.00035 
(g/(mg • h))) was determined. For both process temperatures (298 and 
308 K), decrease in the parameter k2 value was observed, along with a 
change in the initial concentration 10 → 50 mg/dm3. Additionally, a 
stronger effect of the decrease corresponded to the higher temperature. 
The theoretical framework of the sorption process, as outlined by the 
PSO kinetic model, suggested that the rate-controlling step involved the 
surface integration reaction of phosphates when interacting with the 
Cs–Ni sorbent. This indicates that the dynamics of adhering phosphates 
to the sorbent surface plays a crucial role in determining the overall 
efficiency of the sorption process.

For the practical application of the new Cs–Ni sorbent type, 
considering its structure and porosity, it is equally important to correctly 
identify the transport phenomena in heterogeneous systems at the 
liquid-solid boundary, as well as the subsequent diffusional mass 
transport mechanisms within porous systems. The PFO and PSO models 
(A.1–2), providing a comprehensive empirical correlation of the sorp-
tion process, do not allow for a more accurate kinetic insight into this 
process. It is especially important to determine the relative influence and 
contribution of the individual resistances to diffusional mass transport, 
associated with the diverse transport processes (both diffusion in the 
surrounding liquid boundary layer and subsequent internal diffusion 
within the pore structure of the chitosan (spongy) polymer matrix). In-
ternal diffusion processes within the Cs–Ni sorbent pore network, with 
a specific distribution of pore diameters (macropores, mesopores, mi-
cropores), are important for the overall sorption rate effect. These are 
represented by several models, e.g. Crank model, Weber & Morris model 
(A.3), and Bangham model [57].

The value of the exponent 0.5 in the W&M model (A.3) results from 
an assumption of Fick’s diffusion model and simplified plate geometry 
(what for the Cs–Ni sorbent based on a polymer of natural origin, 
subjected to surface structure modification, may be quite a large 
simplification). The W&M kinetic model is especially useful to identify 
the kinetic conditions of the intraparticle diffusion processes. The 
characteristic course of measurement data in the linearized system qt = f 
(t1/2) indicates the complexity of the process of identifying changeable 
mass transfer resistance in a porous heterogeneous system. When 
intraparticle diffusion (pore diffusion) is the only process controlling the 
sorption rate, the straight line representing all experimental data in 
linearized coordinates passes through the point (0,0) – i.e. CW&M = 0. In 
other cases, the sorption rate is only partially co-controlled by intra-
particle diffusion (pore diffusion), and therefore also conditioned by 
other co-occurring processes. The linear segments, that characterize 

changes in the dominant phenomena of sorption, allow for the identi-
fication of key mechanism(s) influencing the sorption rate and their 
distribution throughout the entire batch process. The value of CW&M is 
directly proportional to the thickness of the diffusion layer, which rep-
resents the external diffusion resistance affecting the transport of the 
sorbate. This relationship enables straightforward, relative comparisons 
under different process conditions, such as varying sorbate concentra-
tions or process temperature.

Successive linear segments in the W&M model coordinates indicate 
subsequent, distinct stages of mass transport, with decreasing rates. 
These are: relatively fast external mass transports in the boundary layer 
surrounding the sorbent grains; partial direct sorption on the external 
surface; and all phenomena of diffusive mass transport inside the porous 
sorbent structure with systematically decreasing transporting channel 
diameters (macropores, mesopores, micropores), with clearly observed 
systematic diffusion rate gradation, until the final sorption on the active 
place occurs.

The W&M model data enables the identification of the ranges of 
external diffusion (the boundary layer at the liquid-solid boundary), and 
internal diffusion inside the porous sorbent. For a more complete 
description, and thus supplementation of the W&M model data, a set of 
detailed models corresponding to one- (A.4), two- (A.5) and three- 
dimensional diffusion (A.6-A.7) was verified. The calculation results 
are presented as Supporting Information: Table A.1 and A.2, together 
with their theoretical interpretation.

The obtained values of the KEKAM eq. (A.8-A.9) parameters and 
their theoretical interpretation are presented in Supporting Information: 
Table A.3.

3.2.3. Sorption isotherms

The equilibrium studies of the phosphate anions sorption process 
from the aqueous solutions on the Cs–Ni sorbent were done at 293 K, 
assuming a sorbent/solution ratio of 0.5 g/50 cm3 and pH 7. The total 
contact time between the phases was 48 h. The initial concentrations of 
phosphates ranged from 1 up to 1149 mg/dm3.

The experimental data concerning the sorption equilibrium were 
analyzed using the theoretical models of 8 sorption isotherms (A.10- 
A.17): Langmuir, Freundlich, Redlich-Peterson, Jovanovi´c, Hill, Sips, 
Toth, and Khan [58]. The nonlinear regression calculations were done 
using the ORIGIN program, based on the data without linearization. The

The sorption character in this new system was reflected the best by 
the Freundlich (A.11), Redlich-Peterson (A.12), Hill (A.14), Sips (A.15), 
Toth (A.16), and Khan (A.17) isotherms (R2 0.988–0.992). A signifi-
cantly lower quality of the experimental data rendering was identified 
for the Langmuir (A.10) (R2 0.921) and Jovanovi´c (A.13) (R2 0.890) 
isotherms. The maximum sorbate concentrations predicted by the in-
dividual isotherms were: qJ = 55.7707 mg/g (Jovanovi´c) and qm =
62.5343 mg/g (Langmuir). Compared to the measurement data trend 
(monotonic increase), the Langmuir model prediction was closer to the 
actual experimental data.

According to the theoretical assumptions of the Freundlich isotherm, 
the eq. (A.11) applies to a non-ideal and reversible sorption, not limited 
by self-establishing one monolayer of sorbate only [59]. Thus, the 
agreement of the empirical Freundlich isotherm with the laboratory data 
indicated the occurrence of a multilayer sorption on a surface with a 
heterogeneous structure in terms of the sorbate particle binding strength 
differentiation, along with the possibility of surface gradients of sorption 
heat distribution [60]. The value 1/n = 0.4183 < 1 (Table 3) suggested 
the mechanism of chemisorption, while the relation 0 < 1/n < 1 indi-
cated favorable conditions for the process [61]. Moreover, the 1/n value 
close to 0 indicated the increased heterogeneity of the Cs–Ni sorbent 
surface in terms of sorption phenomena.

The correlation of the measurement data (R2 0.989) with the 
Redlich-Peterson isotherm (A.12), representing a hybrid isotherm with 
the features of the Langmuir and the Freundlich isotherms, also inde-
pendently indicated the occurrence of the sorbate spatial distributions 
other than a monolayer. The parameter g = 0.5897 (Table 3) also 
indicated a more complex intermediate state between the full assump-
tions of the Langmuir isotherm (where g = ca. 1) and the scientific 
foundations of the Freundlich isotherm (where g = ca. 0) [62].

The Hill isotherm (A.14), assuming the sorption process as a coop-
erative phenomenon, enables the identification of different binding/ 
sorption sites in structurally complex molecules. It helps to identify 
certain interactions between sorbed molecules. The value of nH =
0.4183 < 1 (Table 3) suggested that the sorption of phosphates onto this 
new sorbent was of a non-cooperative nature. This could be directly 
explained by the relatively simple chemical structure of the phosphate 
anions, without the co-occurrence of numerous different functional 
groups and their potential specific physicochemical interactions.

The Sips isotherm (A.15), also representing a combination of Lang-
muir and Freundlich isotherms, can be used to identify sorption systems 
exhibiting heterogeneity and lack of spatial interactions between sorbed 
molecules. It is usually used to enable correct representation of the 
asymptotic limitation in the surface sorbate concentration, not allowed 
by the original Freundlich isotherm (A.11). At low sorbate concentra-
tions, its form is consistent with the Freundlich isotherm. In the case of 
higher concentrations, the model predicts the formation of one sorbate 
layer and saturation of the available surface, following the predictions of 
the Langmuir isotherm. The Toth isotherm (A.16) is useful for describing 
data corresponding to heterogeneous sorption systems, initially 
assuming an asymmetric quasi-Gaussian energy distribution, with most 
of the active sites showing lower sorption energy concerning the 
extreme of the distribution or the average value resulting from this 
distribution. The data concerning the new original system suggested, 
independently, multilayer and surface heterogeneous sorption of the 
phosphate anions. In comparison, Khan’s isotherm (A.17) can be used as 
a generalized sorption isotherm, especially suggested for pure sorbate 
solutions.

Simultaneous interpretation of the parameters 1/n (in Freundlich 
isotherm A.11) and g (in Redlich-Peterson isotherm A.12) values, indi-
cating on the one hand the chemisorption process, and at the same time 
theoretically allowing some intermediate states between the monolayer 
and multilayer structures of the sorbate, as well as the heterogeneity of 
the surface due to the sorption phenomena, suggested complex physi-
2(cid:0)
(cid:0)
cochemical interactions between the Cs–Ni sorbent and H2PO4
/HPO4

9 

anions. In addition to the dominant chemical interactions between 
different types of phosphate and Ni(II) ions, representing chemisorption 
phenomena, and mainly related to the formation of stable complexes, 
other types of interactions were also identified, such as hydrogen bonds 
(derived from amino groups of chitosan) or purely physical electrostatic 
interactions between the positively charged Cs–Ni hydrogel surface and 
phosphate anions with different dissociation degrees. In particular, the 
indication of an intermediate state (g = 0.5897) between the Langmuir 
and Freundlich models suggested the possibility of the partial occur-
rence of certain monolayer structures of the sorbate on the part of the 
available sorption surface in the spatial Cs–Ni hydrogel by the Lang-
muir isotherm.

The Langmuir isotherm (A.10) is based on a simplified theoretical 
assumption of a single-layer sorbate, and homogeneous sorption process 
exclusively on certain specific active sites of the sorption surface, 
identical energetically and equivalent in terms of potential sorption 
capabilities (equal affinity with respect to the sorbate) [63]. According 
to these assumptions, one molecule is expected to be bound to one active 
site only. The model excludes any mutual interactions between already 
sorbed molecules, including their eventual surface transmigration and 
multilayer sorption phenomena. In relation to the analyzed measure-
ment data, this model showed a slightly lower quality of fit (R2 0.921), 
especially in relation to the higher values of sorbate concentrations in 
the contacted aqueous solution.

Based on the Langmuir sorption isotherm model, Webber and 
Chakravorti introduced a quantity called separation factor (RL), Eq. (8): 

RL =

1
1 + KLC0

(8) 

where KL is the Langmuir constant in (A.10), while C0 represents the 
initial concentration of the sorbate in the contacting solution, mg/dm3. 
Lower values of the RL parameter indicate more favorable sorption 
conditions [64]. Moreover, RL may be conveniently used to identify the 
nature of the sorption process under given conditions – unfavorable (RL 
> 1), linear (RL = 1), favorable (0 < RL < 1) or irreversible process (RL =
0). In the case of the analyzed measurement data (for KL = 0.0055 dm3/ 
mg), the range of the initial concentration was C0 = 12.15 → 1611.43 
mg/dm3, corresponding thus to RL = 0.937 → 0.101 range. This indi-
cated favorable conditions of the sorption, especially more so with the 
increasing sorbate concentration. Such a tendency indirectly suggested 
participation of chemisorption phenomena in the investigated new 
original system of the Cs–Ni sorbent.

Direct evidence for a chemical reaction occurring during the sorption 
of phosphates on the Cs–Ni sorbent was provided by the analysis of 
FTIR and XPS spectra (as discussed in Section 3.1). The studies 
confirmed that the mechanism involved chemical interactions between 
phosphate anions and nickel(II) species, leading to the formation of the 
stable Ni-PO4 complexes. The Jovanovi´c model (A.13) is based on 
similar theoretical assumptions as the Langmuir isotherm (A.10), but it 
also assumes the possibility of some mechanical contacts in the sorbent- 
sorbate system [65]. The relatively low value of the R2 (0.890) suggested 
rather practical absence of such mechanical effects in the Cs–Ni and 
phosphate anions system.

Considering that the sorption character in this new system was best 
reflected by the Freundlich (A.11), Redlich-Peterson (A.12), Hill (A.14), 
Sips (A.15), Toth (A.16), and Khan (A.17) isotherms (R2 0.988–0.992), 
the possibility of independent use of many isotherms with practically the 
same R2 – but each of them based on different theoretical foundations – 
indicated the complexity of the phosphate anions sorption on the new 
studied Cs–Ni sorbent.

Each of the discussed isotherms allows for the analysis of the process 
from a different point of view, highlighting certain details and mecha-
nisms, which provide a coherent and mutually complementary picture 
of this complex separation mechanism. This was the authors’ goal - to 
show the complexity of the analyzed phenomena and the overall

non-ideal and reversible sorption, with the possibility of multilayer 
sorption on a heterogeneous surface, was visible. The chemisorption 
mechanism, suggested by the isotherm model, matched very well the 
independent analytical data indicating, among others, the potential and

3.2.4. Dynamic sorption

Fixed-bed column tests were also done to investigate the techno-
logical performance of the new, original Cs–Ni sorbent as a bed in the 
potential practical working environment. Moreover, the authors 
compared the effectiveness of the Cs–Ni sorbent in removing phos-
phates with unmodified chitosan (Cs) in identical fixed-bed mode. 
Fig. A.2 in the Appendix presents the experimental breakthrough curves 
for both sorbent types, directly indicating the differences. Technical 
details of the tests and the comparison of the total capacity of phos-
phates with other available sorbents in the column study approach 
(Table A.5) are described in the Supporting Information. The gained 
technical data demonstrated the advantageous potential of the new 
Cs–Ni sorbent for the continuous separation of phosphates from 
aqueous solutions (as a parallel system of a few (e.g., 2–3) cascades). On 
the contrary, the Cs sorbent (without any modification) demonstrated a 
breakthrough point too early. Moreover, its relatively low durability 
may be a potential technical problem concerning exploitation of 
industrial-scale sorption columns.

The sorption calculations given in Table 4 showed that the Cs–Ni 
system was an effective sorbent for the removal of phosphate anions 
from water. The total dynamic sorption capacity for the Cs and Cs–Ni 
sorbents was 2.45 and 4.98 mg/g, respectively. These results confirmed 
that the addition of nickel ions improved the sorption of phosphates 
from the aqueous solution.

For the unmodified chitosan sorbent (Cs), the breakthrough times 
(tb) were identified, indicated by the outlet concentration from the 
column (Ct) reaching 10 % and 50 % of the inlet concentration (C0). 
These values were approximately 30 and 60 min, respectively. The 
exhaustion time (ts), defined as the moment when the outlet concen-
tration (Ct) after passing through the sorption column reaches 95 % of 
the inlet concentration (C0), was ca. 255 min.

The use of the new chitosan bed, enhanced by the introduction of Ni 
into the hydrogel structure, led to significant changes in the experi-
mental breakthrough curve course. Notably, the bed reached saturation 
much more slowly, which extended its operational time between 
regeneration steps. Both values of the breakthrough time (tb) were much 
longer, reaching 75 min (for 10 % of C0) and 255 min (for 50 % of C0), 
which was a 4.5-fold and over 3.6-fold extension compared to the 
reference data (for Cs). Due to the significant slowdown of the bed 
saturating process starting from 450 min of the process time, it was not 
possible to experimentally determine the bed exhaustion time (for 95 % 
of C0) more precisely, omitting the uncertain extrapolation calculations 
concerning the identified nonlinear trend for the highest values of the 
process time. Despite not reaching sorbent saturation (i.e. Ct/C0 < 1) 
during first 600 min, analogous nomenclature was used in the termi-
nology for clarity (total dynamic sorption capacity).

3.2.5. Phosphate sorption mechanism

Based on the interpretation of FTIR and XPS analyses, as well as on 
the relevant literature (e.g. [66]), it was found that the reactive func-
tional groups in chitosan, particularly the -OH groups with free electron 
pairs, coordinated with Ni2+
ions. During the preparation of the 
hydrogel granules, chitosan was gelled together with the dissolved 
nickel salt in a 10 % NaOH aqueous solution. This process fixed the 
hydrogel structure, on which Ni(OH)₂ particles formed (see Fig. 3 Panel 
3d). As a result, nickel hydroxide was advantageously dispersed and 
incorporated into the chitosan backbone through the hydroxyl groups. 
The binding of nickel ions did not involve the -NH2 groups, as confirmed 
by the analytical results of FTIR and XPS studies (see Table 1 and Fig. 3
Panel 3c, respectively). The proposed structure of the modified chitosan 
molecule is illustrated on the left side in Fig. 5.

The results of studies on phosphates sorption showed that the use of 
the new technology for preparing hydrogels significantly increased the 
efficiency of the process. It turned out that the modification of chitosan 
with nickel(II) led to the formation of the new original Cs–Ni sorbent, 
enhancing the sorption properties of the natural chitosan towards anions 
in slightly acidic, neutral or slightly alkaline solutions. This enhance-
ment was attributed to an increase in the point of zero charge (pHPZC) of 
the sorbent. Specifically, the introduction of Ni2+
ions into the chitosan 
skeleton increased the pHPZC value to 8.1, in comparison to the 7.4–7.5 
range characteristic of non-modified chitosan [67,68].

The pHPZC is a crucial parameter for removing phosphates from 
natural waters, typically exhibiting neutral or slightly alkaline pH. 
Therefore, the modification of the chitosan surface with Ni(OH)2 elim-
inates the need for the preliminary pH adjustment to acidic levels when 
using the non-modified one. Consequently, it is an environmentally 
friendly advantage resulting from limitation of chemical reagents con-
sumption in separation technologies.

The study also identified and explained the effect of pH on phos-
phates sorption, indicating directly the possibility of desorbing accu-
mulated phosphates in alkaline solutions.

The research confirmed the potential of the novel Cs–Ni sorbent for 
ionic phosphorus forms removal from water and provided theoretical 
insights into the mechanisms by which phosphorus can be more effec-
tively separated. The proposed mechanisms aligned with the results 
based on the FTIR and XPS analyses, batch sorption experiments, and 
available scientific literature. This study confirmed that nickel existed 
within the chitosan structure in the chemical form of Ni(OH)2, binding 
to the chitosan via primary and secondary hydroxyl groups. The free 
amino group formed weak Van der Waals and electrostatic interactions 
(depending on the solution’s pH) with phosphate anions.

The new original sorbent showed significant application potential, 
and the results from the column tests confirmed its effectiveness in 
revitalizing the P-contaminated waters.

4. Conclusions

CRediT authorship contribution statement

An innovative technological method for sorption-based removal of 
phosphorus compounds from aqueous solutions using a novel composite 
structure based on modified chitosan with nickel(II) ions (Cs–Ni) was 
presented. Purposeful and focused modification of the non-crosslinked 
chitosan (Cs) with nickel(II) ions advantageously enhanced its phys-
ical and chemical sorption properties for phosphate anions separation. 
This improvement was indicated by the increase in the pH value at the 
point of zero charge and the strong chemical affinity of Ni(II) ions to-
wards various phosphates.

Additionally, the Cs–Ni sorbent demonstrated greater mechanical 
durability and improved resistance to varying chemical conditions 
compared to the unmodified chitosan, eliminating the need for further 
cross-linking. From a kinetic viewpoint, the phosphates sorption data for 
the Cs–Ni sorbent corresponded to the pseudo-second-order model. The 
Weber & Morris kinetic model indicated more detailed, intraparticle 
diffusion-based multi-mechanism process.

The significantly enhanced phosphates sorption properties of the 
Cs–Ni sorbent can be attributed to three main properties of chitosan: 

1. Its ability to be structurally modified with dispersed nickel ions, 

introducing to the system two advantages: 
– a relatively high specific surface area,
– strong affinity for phosphate ions,

Nickel(II)-modified chitosan hydrogel: A hybrid sorbent for removal of 
phosphate anions from water

Łukasz Wujcicki a, Tomasz Ma´ndok b, Dominika Paprota b, Krzysztof Piotrowski c, 
Jakub Adamek d, Maciej Krzywiecki e, Karolina Goc f, Joanna Kluczka a,*
a Department of Inorganic, Analytical Chemistry and Electrochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Silesian University of Technology, B. Krzywoustego 6, 44-100 Gliwice, 
Poland
b Faculty of Chemistry, Silesian University of Technology, ks. M. Strzody 9, 44-100 Gliwice, Poland
c Department of Chemical Engineering and Process Design, Faculty of Chemistry, Silesian University of Technology, ks. M. Strzody 7, 44-100 Gliwice, Poland
d Department of Organic Chemistry, Bioorganic Chemistry and Biotechnology, Faculty of Chemistry, Silesian University of Technology, B. Krzywoustego 8, 44-100 
Gliwice, Poland
e Institute of Physics - Centre for Science and Education, Silesian University of Technology, Konarskiego 22B, 44-100 Gliwice, Poland
f Łukasiewicz Research Network-Institute of Non-Ferrous Metals, Sowi´nskiego 5, 44-100 Gliwice, Poland

A R T I C L E I N F O

A B S T R A C T

Keywords:
Chitosan
Phosphate anions removal
Sorption

A novel nickel-modified chitosan sorbent (Cs–Ni), highly efficient in phosphates removal from aqueous solu-
tions, was proposed. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and point of 
zero charges of sorbent were employed to determine its structure and properties. Batch sorption experiments 
allowed to identify the Cs–Ni high phosphate sorption capacity, exceeding 62.5 mg/g (with the removal effi-
ciency of 73 % - for optimal experimental conditions: pH 7, sorbent dose 10 g/dm3, temperature 293 K, contact 
time 1440 min). The equilibrium data for the new Cs–Ni sorbent and aqueous solution of phosphates fitted well 
with the Freundlich, Redlich-Peterson, Hill, Sips, Toth, and Khan sorption isotherms (R2 = 0.988–0.992), sug-
gesting sorbate distributions other than a creation of monolayer on the heterogeneous sorption surface. The 
phosphates sorption on the new sorbent resulted from the electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged 
(cid:0)
anions and the positively charged Cs–Ni surface, formation of stable complexes between these 
H2PO4
anions and nickel(II) species, and the anions direct sorption by interacting with the free and active chitosan 
+
OPO(OH)2). The results highlighted the promising potential 
amino groups through hydrogen bonding (-NH3
of the Cs–Ni hydrogel for phosphate anions removal from contaminated water.

1. Introduction

Surface and groundwater pollution is a significant, common, and 
complex environmental problem, which is becoming more and more 
important nowadays [1]. One of the key pollutants that deteriorate 
water quality is phosphorus compounds, originating from both the 
natural phenomena of biomass decomposition and the wastewater from 
various technological processes or agricultural fertilization. The most 
prevalent forms of phosphorus in wastewater are phosphate anions [2]. 
High concentration of phosphates (PO₄3(cid:0)
) in the surface water is closely 

related to eutrophication - a phenomenon unfavorable for the biosphere, 
resulting in excessive aquatic vegetation and consequently, its slow 
biodegradation, which negatively affects the biodiversity and strongly 
disturbs the stability of aqueous ecosystems [3].

The primary natural sources of phosphorus are phosphate rocks, 
which are considered to be a non-renewable resource [4]. They are 
however intensively exploited for a production of phosphoric and 
superphosphoric acids – main market substrates necessary for mineral 
fertilizers, and animal feed supplements manufacture [5]. This common 
in agricultural 
use of phosphorus supplements and 

fertilizers

phosphate anions sorption; FTIR, Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy; XPS, X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy; ICP-OES, Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical 
Emission Spectroscopy; P-PO4, Phosphorus concentration calculated based on phosphates concentration; pHPZC, pH value corresponding to the point of zero charge of 
sorbent; PFO, Pseudo-first order kinetic model; PSO, Pseudo-second order kinetic model.

water bodies, as well as in wastewater, where, for example, the average 
3(cid:0)
) varies from 4 to 20 mg/dm3 [6–8]. 
concentration of phosphates (PO4
However, from another point of view, the same wastewater is starting to 
be seen as a secondary, and in the future perhaps the only available, 
source of phosphorus. For this reason, it is necessary to identify and 
continually technologically improve effective methods for separation 
and recycling of phosphates from the aquatic environment, taking into 
account in particular the problem of dilution of phosphorus compounds, 
co-existence of their different chemical forms, as well as co-presence of 
other chemical species in complex physicochemical systems of waste-
water, derived from different sources [9] (Fig. 1).

One promising approach for not only efficient, but also possibly se-
lective separation of phosphate pollutants is based on using various 
sorbents [10–13]. In recent years, some new hydrogel-based materials 
are the subject of research due to the possibility of a practical use of a 
number of their new separation capabilities, resulting directly from their 
specific chemical structure, and the advantageous physical properties in 
the field of controlled separation mechanisms. The following properties 
of these systems are particularly useful: developed large sorption sur-
face, ability to swell in water, and possibilities of directed “corrections” 
of surface chemistry [14–16]. Among the hydrogels based on natural 
compounds, chitosan, a polysaccharide derived from chitin, is the sub-
ject of intense laboratory tests [17–19].

Chitosan demonstrates many unique and advantageous properties, 
like biocompatibility, biodegradability, and low toxicity. In particular, a 
possibility of a formation of surface complexes with various specially 
selected anions and cations can lead to unique microstructural concepts 
and synergistic solutions in terms of increased possibilities of effective 
and selective sorption of different compounds. This makes it a very 
convenient and modifiable matrix for tailored sorption materials for-
mation [20,21]. Chitosan hydrogels, doped on the surface with appro-
priate active ingredients, can be used with a high flexibility, playing the 
role of composite materials with catalytic, adsorption or absorption 
abilities, designed towards certain pollutants removal [22,23]. The use 
of such modified chitosan to eliminate phosphate anions from 

wastewater can enable much more efficient separation of phosphorus 
compounds and their subsequent technological recovery. This includes, 
however, optimal designing of composite materials, while considering 
not only identified chemical affinity, but also adequate spatial disper-
sion of active centres, closely related to large enough specific surface 
area of the interphase contact.

Chemical modifications of chitosan matrix, such as the dispersion of 
chemically active metal forms in the chitosan skeleton, can significantly 
enhance its sorption capacity towards phosphates, due to the increase in 
the number of co-acting separating mechanisms [24–27]. An under-
standing of the sorption mechanisms, their interactions, and suscepti-
bility to the influence of various factors controlling the reaction can 
make the optimization of phosphorus removal processes [28,29]. Met-
al–chitosan composites are regarded to be materials relatively simple to 
manufacture [30]. Introduction of metals, such as lanthanum [31], 
zirconium [32], iron [33], magnesium [34], and calcium [35] into the 
chitosan matrix was reported in the literature. Ni2+
ions dispersed 
within the chitosan hydrogel matrix can potentially form numerous 
sparingly soluble compounds with (PO4)3(cid:0)
or many stable complexes, 
also with other species of phosphorus present in the wastewater, like 
, with the Ni2+
, (P3O9)3(cid:0)
(P2O7)4(cid:0)
complex showing 
the greatest stability [36].

-(P2O7)4(cid:0)

On the other hand, some papers are available that use phosphorus 
compounds to bind Ni2+
from aqueous solutions. The authors’ chemical 
concept was thus on the reverse binding mechanism. Examples include 
works on nickel sorption on different phosphorus forms, like titanium 
phosphate [37], focusing on high capacity for nickel binding, which is 
indirectly related to the strong interactions during the formation of a 
stable, sparingly soluble nickel phosphate Ni3(PO4)2 (pKsp = 30.3) [36]. 
The stable interactions formed between the Ni2+
cation and the 
(P3O10)5(cid:0)
anion were used in a subsequent study [38], where nickel ions 
adsorbed on chitosan cross-linked with tripolyphosphate.

In this work, an original chemical concept of integrating nickel with 
chitosan hydrogel matrix to develop a novel sorbent for the separation of 
phosphorus from an aqueous solution was experimentally verified in 
batch and dynamic systems. This research covered both sorption kinetics

 Ł. Wujcicki et al. 

International Journal of Biological Macromolecules 322 (2025) 146994 

and equilibrium (isotherms), along with a comprehensive analysis of a 
phosphate’s sorption mechanism. A hypothesis in the study was that 
nickel ions could actively participate in binding phosphate ions from the 
solution. A potential synergistic effect between chitosan in its hydrogel 
form and Ni metal ions within its structure was assumed. The chitosan 
hydrogel should, due to its swelling capacity, especially facilitate spatial 
nickel dispersion and advantageously increase the exposition of Ni- 
based active sites within a flexibly spongy structure, thus improving 
the phosphorous binding efficiency. Similar solution, where nickel was 
added to the chitosan matrix, was used for efficient removal of borates 
from an aqueous solution [39]. Chemical conditions, or to be more 
precise phosphate affinity, directed authors’ attention to nickel ions, 
which were not previously used in the system for phosphate sorption.

2. Experimental section

2.1. Chemicals and instruments

2.1.1. Chemicals

All reagents were used without additional purification and in their 
commercially available forms. Deionized water: supported by Millipore 
Elix 10 system with a conductivity of 0.067 μS/cm was used. Potassium 
dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (CAS 
number: 7778-77-0). Standard solutions of phosphates (1000 mg/dm3 
P-PO4, code number: 104690) and nickel (1000 mg/dm3 Ni, code 
number: 119792) were provided by Merck. Chitosan, HMW, high 
viscous, with a molecular weight of 600–800 kDa (the viscosity test 
determined the molecular weight to be 717 kDa using Ubbelohde 
capillary) was used in the experiments. Its degree of deacetylation (DD) 
determined previously [40], using the H1 NMR method, was 96.58 % 
and was provided by Acros Organics (code number: 428855000). Other 
reagents used in the synthesis of the sorbent were as follows: nickel(II) 
chloride (Merck, CAS number: 7718-54-9), acetic acid (Avantor Per-
formance Materials Poland S.A., CAS number: 64–19-7), and sodium 
hydroxide (Chempur, CAS number: 1310-58-3). The analytical reagent 
grade was used as a standard for all chemical materials applied.

2.1.2. Analytical methods and instruments

Liquid phases analyses were done using the ICP-OES method on the 
following analytical lines - for P: λ = 213.618 nm, λ = 214.914 nm, λ =
177.434 nm, λ = 178.222 nm, and for Ni: λ = 216.555 nm, λ = 231.604 
nm, λ = 231.604 nm, λ = 230.299 nm. A calibration curves range of 
0.1–50 mg/dm3 was established using the Varian 710-ES spectropho-
tometer. The FTIR analysis was carried out using a Spectrum Two 
spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Waltham). The XPS measurements were 
performed in an ultra-high vacuum (UHV) multi-chamber equipped 
with PREVAC EA15 hemispherical electron energy analyzer and with a 
2D-MCP detector (Prevac Sp. z o.o.), operating at a base pressure of 
(cid:0) 9 Pa. The monochromatic X-ray source (PREVAC dual-anode XR- 
9⋅10
40B, Al-Kα excitation line with energy of 1486.60 eV) was used for 
sample excitation. The pass energy (PE) was set to 200 eV for survey 
spectra (0.8 eV scanning step) and to 100 eV for high-resolution scans of 
selected energy regions (0.05 eV scanning step). All measurements were 
based on a normal take-off angle and a curved analyzer exit slit (0.8 ×
25 mm) to achieve the highest energy resolution and to minimize 
diffraction effects. The binding energy (BE) scale was calibrated using 
Au 4f7/2 (84.0 eV) region. The raw spectral data were processed and 
automatically directly interpreted by CASA XPS® software (version 
2.3.23PR01). The integration of Gauss (30 %) and Lorenz (70 %) func-
tions was performed and combined together with the Shirley function to 
minimize background effect. For viscosity tests, an Ubbelohde (K = 0.5, 
50–500 mm2/s) capillary (CONBEST, Krak´ow, Poland) was used (0.2 M 
CH3COOH and 0.1 M CH3COONa, chitosan concentrations 0.2–1 g/dm3, 
303 K). The chitosan molecular weight was calculated according to the 
Mark–Houwink equation (k = 0.01001 cm3/g, α = 0.8349) [41].

2.2. Cs–Ni sorbent fabrication

The procedure described previously [42] was adopted for this 
research to prepare the chitosan granules modified with nickel. In a 500 
cm3 conical flask, 5 g of powdered chitosan was dissolved in 250 cm3 of 
1 % CH3COOH aqueous solution, using a magnetic stirrer for mixing. 
Subsequently, 1.1041 g of solid nickel(II) chloride was introduced, to 
achieve a nickel-to-chitosan mass ratio of 1:10, and dissolved under 
continuous stirring. The prepared mixture was then transferred drop-by- 
drop into a 10 % NaOH aqueous solution using a syringe for coagulation 
and granules formation. The formed sorbent granules were washed with 
deionized water until a neutral pH and low conductivity (< 10 μS/cm) in 
the post-processed effluents were reached.

Chemical composition of the sorbent was determined using the ICP- 
OES method. The sorbent granules were dried in atmospheric air to 
eliminate moisture and then dissolved in a concentrated HNO3. The 
mineralizate was diluted 100 times for quantitative analysis. For 
comparative qualitative and quantitative analysis, the manufactured 
materials were investigated before and after sorption. Samples for FTIR 
and XPS analyses were prepared by CHRIST Alpha 1-2LDplus freeze- 
dryer, following manual pulverization in an agate mortar. The XPS 
and FTIR analyses corresponded to sorbents with a nickel-to-polymer 
ratio of 1:2, correlating with 50 wt% of nickel fraction within the dry 
sorbent (333 mg/g).

2.3. Sorption pH and pHPZC study

The influence of solution pH in a range of 4–10 on the sorption 
process was investigated using KH2PO4 solution (10 mg/dm3 P-PO4). 
The solution pH was adjusted by an addition of 0.1 M aqueous solutions 
of HCl or NaOH. Then, the Cs–Ni granules samples, 0.5 g each, after 
draining with blotting paper, were introduced into 250 cm3 conical 
flasks. Each vessel was filled with 50 cm3 of the solution, sealed with 
ground glass stoppers and shaken at 293 K for 24 h at 180 rpm 
(CHEMLAND, SK-O330-PRO). Then, the Cs–Ni sorbent granules were 
separated using analytical filters (FILTRAK, hard filters, type 390, 84 g/ 
m2). Chemical compositions of both filtrates and initial solutions were 
identified using the ICP-OES method (each analytical test was done 
twice). Sorption capacity of the Cs–Ni sorbent (q) was calculated by Eq. 
(1): 

q =

The point of zero charge (PZC) was determined alongside with the

before and after the sorption, and the PZC corresponded to the point

the pHPZC.

2.7. Moisture content study

(7) 

The moisture content within the Cs–Ni sorbent structure, required 
for calculating mass balance, was determined using a moisture analyzer 
(RADWAG MA 200.3Y). The ca. 1 g of sorbent (in the form of hydrogel 
granules) was first drained with blotting paper, followed by an auto-
matic procedure implemented in the moisture analyzer. After drying 
time (ca. 30 min) determined automatically by the analyzer (stable 
constant mass detection), the results were recorded.

3. Results and discussion

3.1. Characterization of the Cs–Ni sorbent

2.6. Dynamic sorption study

3.1.1. FTIR analysis

The dynamic phosphate anions sorption tests were carried out in a 
laboratory glass column (15 mm inner diameter, 150 mm height) packed 
with ca. 10 g of the Cs–Ni sorbent. The basic technical parameters of the 
sorption, such as bed height, bed density, as well as other more specific 
information were provided in the Appendix. A peristaltic pump 
(LAMBDA Preciflow) was used to maintain a pseudo-continuous inflow 
rate of 1 cm3/min at the top of the column, allowing then for the 
aqueous phosphate solution to flow downward through the bed voids. 
To determine the sorption breakthrough curve, effluent samples of 15 
cm3 each were systematically collected from the bottom of the column 
every 15 min using Falcon tubes placed in an automatic collector 
(Kamush, LPfrac030). All collected liquid fractions were subsequently 
analyzed using the ICP-OES method. The dynamic sorption experiments 
were performed twice to verify the repeatability of the data.

3.1.2. XPS analysis

Further insight into the characteristics of the new Ni–Cs sorbents 
was done employing X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Survey spectra 
(not shown for brevity) presented all expected energy regions (i.e. C 1 s, 
N 1 s and O 1 s for all the samples and additional Ni 2p energy region for 
Cs–Ni and Cs-Ni-P ones). For the last sample, representing Cs–Ni sor-
bent after phosphates sorption, P 2p energy region was also shown. For 
the detailed description of the bonding mechanism, and as a support for

decomposition. The common parts for all the samples decomposed re-

the majority was decomposed into four main components assigned for 
C–C/C–H configuration (~284.6 eV), and C-N-O/C-NH/C-O-C con-
figurations [49]. The multiple assignment rationalized the overall 
component intensity with respect to the primary C–C one. The last two 
components should be ascribed to C––O/C-OH and C-OOH, respectively. 
However, the fourth one was visible only for Cs–Ni sample [50]. The 
last one was most likely a result of the unwanted atmosphere species

3.2. Phosphate anions sorption

3.2.1. pH effect

The initial solution pH influence on the phosphates sorption from the 
aqueous solutions using the Cs–Ni sorbent is graphically demonstrated 
in Fig. 4a. The results indicated its significant influence on the sorption 
effects. When the pH increased, the sorbent ability (quantified by its 
capacity parameter) to bind phosphates decreased. A particularly sig-
nificant effect of sorption capacity drop was experimentally identified 
for pH range ≥ 8. This phenomenon might be explained by two main

active sites. On the contrary, the PFO kinetic model is based on an 
assumption of an existence of only a few active sites within its structure. 
The compatibility of the PSO kinetic with the experimental data was also 
consistent with the independent analytical information about large 
structural development of potential sorption surface with active sites 
(both from Ni dispersion, as well as derived directly from the intrinsic 
chemical structure of chitosan surface). It was also suggested by a higher 
correlation to Freundlich isotherm, based on an assumption of a het-
erogeneous surface and multilayer sorption effects.

At a process temperature of 298 K, increasing the initial concentra-
tion of phosphates from c1 = 10 up to c2 = 50 mg/dm3 led to significant 
changes in the equilibrium concentration of the sorbate. This concen-
tration rose from approximately 7.53 mg/g to about 11.81 mg/g, which 
aligned closely with the measured data of around 7.43 and ca. 11.44 
mg/g. The values of the k2 parameter of the PSO model also indicated 
change in the process rate, especially in its first phase (k2 = 0.0015 → 
0.00056 (g/(mg • h))). Increasing the process temperature up to 308 K 
caused different changes in both q2 and k2 parameters. For the higher 
process temperature, a similar trend in the q2 parameter was observed, 
along with a five-fold change in the initial concentration of the sorbate 
(q2 ca. 7.90 → 21.15 mg/g), but this change was almost three-fold. The 
k2 parameter value for the higher temperature was almost identical to 
that at 298 K for a lower initial concentration. For a concentration of 50 
mg/dm3, a significant decrease in the k2 parameter (0.00056 → 0.00035 
(g/(mg • h))) was determined. For both process temperatures (298 and 
308 K), decrease in the parameter k2 value was observed, along with a 
change in the initial concentration 10 → 50 mg/dm3. Additionally, a 
stronger effect of the decrease corresponded to the higher temperature. 
The theoretical framework of the sorption process, as outlined by the 
PSO kinetic model, suggested that the rate-controlling step involved the 
surface integration reaction of phosphates when interacting with the 
Cs–Ni sorbent. This indicates that the dynamics of adhering phosphates 
to the sorbent surface plays a crucial role in determining the overall 
efficiency of the sorption process.

For the practical application of the new Cs–Ni sorbent type, 
considering its structure and porosity, it is equally important to correctly 
identify the transport phenomena in heterogeneous systems at the 
liquid-solid boundary, as well as the subsequent diffusional mass 
transport mechanisms within porous systems. The PFO and PSO models 
(A.1–2), providing a comprehensive empirical correlation of the sorp-
tion process, do not allow for a more accurate kinetic insight into this 
process. It is especially important to determine the relative influence and 
contribution of the individual resistances to diffusional mass transport, 
associated with the diverse transport processes (both diffusion in the 
surrounding liquid boundary layer and subsequent internal diffusion 
within the pore structure of the chitosan (spongy) polymer matrix). In-
ternal diffusion processes within the Cs–Ni sorbent pore network, with 
a specific distribution of pore diameters (macropores, mesopores, mi-
cropores), are important for the overall sorption rate effect. These are 
represented by several models, e.g. Crank model, Weber & Morris model 
(A.3), and Bangham model [57].

The value of the exponent 0.5 in the W&M model (A.3) results from 
an assumption of Fick’s diffusion model and simplified plate geometry 
(what for the Cs–Ni sorbent based on a polymer of natural origin, 
subjected to surface structure modification, may be quite a large 
simplification). The W&M kinetic model is especially useful to identify 
the kinetic conditions of the intraparticle diffusion processes. The 
characteristic course of measurement data in the linearized system qt = f 
(t1/2) indicates the complexity of the process of identifying changeable 
mass transfer resistance in a porous heterogeneous system. When 
intraparticle diffusion (pore diffusion) is the only process controlling the 
sorption rate, the straight line representing all experimental data in 
linearized coordinates passes through the point (0,0) – i.e. CW&M = 0. In 
other cases, the sorption rate is only partially co-controlled by intra-
particle diffusion (pore diffusion), and therefore also conditioned by 
other co-occurring processes. The linear segments, that characterize 

changes in the dominant phenomena of sorption, allow for the identi-
fication of key mechanism(s) influencing the sorption rate and their 
distribution throughout the entire batch process. The value of CW&M is 
directly proportional to the thickness of the diffusion layer, which rep-
resents the external diffusion resistance affecting the transport of the 
sorbate. This relationship enables straightforward, relative comparisons 
under different process conditions, such as varying sorbate concentra-
tions or process temperature.

Successive linear segments in the W&M model coordinates indicate 
subsequent, distinct stages of mass transport, with decreasing rates. 
These are: relatively fast external mass transports in the boundary layer 
surrounding the sorbent grains; partial direct sorption on the external 
surface; and all phenomena of diffusive mass transport inside the porous 
sorbent structure with systematically decreasing transporting channel 
diameters (macropores, mesopores, micropores), with clearly observed 
systematic diffusion rate gradation, until the final sorption on the active 
place occurs.

The W&M model data enables the identification of the ranges of 
external diffusion (the boundary layer at the liquid-solid boundary), and 
internal diffusion inside the porous sorbent. For a more complete 
description, and thus supplementation of the W&M model data, a set of 
detailed models corresponding to one- (A.4), two- (A.5) and three- 
dimensional diffusion (A.6-A.7) was verified. The calculation results 
are presented as Supporting Information: Table A.1 and A.2, together 
with their theoretical interpretation.

The obtained values of the KEKAM eq. (A.8-A.9) parameters and 
their theoretical interpretation are presented in Supporting Information: 
Table A.3.

3.2.3. Sorption isotherms

The equilibrium studies of the phosphate anions sorption process 
from the aqueous solutions on the Cs–Ni sorbent were done at 293 K, 
assuming a sorbent/solution ratio of 0.5 g/50 cm3 and pH 7. The total 
contact time between the phases was 48 h. The initial concentrations of 
phosphates ranged from 1 up to 1149 mg/dm3.

The experimental data concerning the sorption equilibrium were 
analyzed using the theoretical models of 8 sorption isotherms (A.10- 
A.17): Langmuir, Freundlich, Redlich-Peterson, Jovanovi´c, Hill, Sips, 
Toth, and Khan [58]. The nonlinear regression calculations were done 
using the ORIGIN program, based on the data without linearization. The

The sorption character in this new system was reflected the best by 
the Freundlich (A.11), Redlich-Peterson (A.12), Hill (A.14), Sips (A.15), 
Toth (A.16), and Khan (A.17) isotherms (R2 0.988–0.992). A signifi-
cantly lower quality of the experimental data rendering was identified 
for the Langmuir (A.10) (R2 0.921) and Jovanovi´c (A.13) (R2 0.890) 
isotherms. The maximum sorbate concentrations predicted by the in-
dividual isotherms were: qJ = 55.7707 mg/g (Jovanovi´c) and qm =
62.5343 mg/g (Langmuir). Compared to the measurement data trend 
(monotonic increase), the Langmuir model prediction was closer to the 
actual experimental data.

According to the theoretical assumptions of the Freundlich isotherm, 
the eq. (A.11) applies to a non-ideal and reversible sorption, not limited 
by self-establishing one monolayer of sorbate only [59]. Thus, the 
agreement of the empirical Freundlich isotherm with the laboratory data 
indicated the occurrence of a multilayer sorption on a surface with a 
heterogeneous structure in terms of the sorbate particle binding strength 
differentiation, along with the possibility of surface gradients of sorption 
heat distribution [60]. The value 1/n = 0.4183 < 1 (Table 3) suggested 
the mechanism of chemisorption, while the relation 0 < 1/n < 1 indi-
cated favorable conditions for the process [61]. Moreover, the 1/n value 
close to 0 indicated the increased heterogeneity of the Cs–Ni sorbent 
surface in terms of sorption phenomena.

The correlation of the measurement data (R2 0.989) with the 
Redlich-Peterson isotherm (A.12), representing a hybrid isotherm with 
the features of the Langmuir and the Freundlich isotherms, also inde-
pendently indicated the occurrence of the sorbate spatial distributions 
other than a monolayer. The parameter g = 0.5897 (Table 3) also 
indicated a more complex intermediate state between the full assump-
tions of the Langmuir isotherm (where g = ca. 1) and the scientific 
foundations of the Freundlich isotherm (where g = ca. 0) [62].

The Hill isotherm (A.14), assuming the sorption process as a coop-
erative phenomenon, enables the identification of different binding/ 
sorption sites in structurally complex molecules. It helps to identify 
certain interactions between sorbed molecules. The value of nH =
0.4183 < 1 (Table 3) suggested that the sorption of phosphates onto this 
new sorbent was of a non-cooperative nature. This could be directly 
explained by the relatively simple chemical structure of the phosphate 
anions, without the co-occurrence of numerous different functional 
groups and their potential specific physicochemical interactions.

The Sips isotherm (A.15), also representing a combination of Lang-
muir and Freundlich isotherms, can be used to identify sorption systems 
exhibiting heterogeneity and lack of spatial interactions between sorbed 
molecules. It is usually used to enable correct representation of the 
asymptotic limitation in the surface sorbate concentration, not allowed 
by the original Freundlich isotherm (A.11). At low sorbate concentra-
tions, its form is consistent with the Freundlich isotherm. In the case of 
higher concentrations, the model predicts the formation of one sorbate 
layer and saturation of the available surface, following the predictions of 
the Langmuir isotherm. The Toth isotherm (A.16) is useful for describing 
data corresponding to heterogeneous sorption systems, initially 
assuming an asymmetric quasi-Gaussian energy distribution, with most 
of the active sites showing lower sorption energy concerning the 
extreme of the distribution or the average value resulting from this 
distribution. The data concerning the new original system suggested, 
independently, multilayer and surface heterogeneous sorption of the 
phosphate anions. In comparison, Khan’s isotherm (A.17) can be used as 
a generalized sorption isotherm, especially suggested for pure sorbate 
solutions.

Simultaneous interpretation of the parameters 1/n (in Freundlich 
isotherm A.11) and g (in Redlich-Peterson isotherm A.12) values, indi-
cating on the one hand the chemisorption process, and at the same time 
theoretically allowing some intermediate states between the monolayer 
and multilayer structures of the sorbate, as well as the heterogeneity of 
the surface due to the sorption phenomena, suggested complex physi-
2(cid:0)
(cid:0)
cochemical interactions between the Cs–Ni sorbent and H2PO4
/HPO4

9 

anions. In addition to the dominant chemical interactions between 
different types of phosphate and Ni(II) ions, representing chemisorption 
phenomena, and mainly related to the formation of stable complexes, 
other types of interactions were also identified, such as hydrogen bonds 
(derived from amino groups of chitosan) or purely physical electrostatic 
interactions between the positively charged Cs–Ni hydrogel surface and 
phosphate anions with different dissociation degrees. In particular, the 
indication of an intermediate state (g = 0.5897) between the Langmuir 
and Freundlich models suggested the possibility of the partial occur-
rence of certain monolayer structures of the sorbate on the part of the 
available sorption surface in the spatial Cs–Ni hydrogel by the Lang-
muir isotherm.

The Langmuir isotherm (A.10) is based on a simplified theoretical 
assumption of a single-layer sorbate, and homogeneous sorption process 
exclusively on certain specific active sites of the sorption surface, 
identical energetically and equivalent in terms of potential sorption 
capabilities (equal affinity with respect to the sorbate) [63]. According 
to these assumptions, one molecule is expected to be bound to one active 
site only. The model excludes any mutual interactions between already 
sorbed molecules, including their eventual surface transmigration and 
multilayer sorption phenomena. In relation to the analyzed measure-
ment data, this model showed a slightly lower quality of fit (R2 0.921), 
especially in relation to the higher values of sorbate concentrations in 
the contacted aqueous solution.

Based on the Langmuir sorption isotherm model, Webber and 
Chakravorti introduced a quantity called separation factor (RL), Eq. (8): 

RL =

1
1 + KLC0

(8) 

where KL is the Langmuir constant in (A.10), while C0 represents the 
initial concentration of the sorbate in the contacting solution, mg/dm3. 
Lower values of the RL parameter indicate more favorable sorption 
conditions [64]. Moreover, RL may be conveniently used to identify the 
nature of the sorption process under given conditions – unfavorable (RL 
> 1), linear (RL = 1), favorable (0 < RL < 1) or irreversible process (RL =
0). In the case of the analyzed measurement data (for KL = 0.0055 dm3/ 
mg), the range of the initial concentration was C0 = 12.15 → 1611.43 
mg/dm3, corresponding thus to RL = 0.937 → 0.101 range. This indi-
cated favorable conditions of the sorption, especially more so with the 
increasing sorbate concentration. Such a tendency indirectly suggested 
participation of chemisorption phenomena in the investigated new 
original system of the Cs–Ni sorbent.

Direct evidence for a chemical reaction occurring during the sorption 
of phosphates on the Cs–Ni sorbent was provided by the analysis of 
FTIR and XPS spectra (as discussed in Section 3.1). The studies 
confirmed that the mechanism involved chemical interactions between 
phosphate anions and nickel(II) species, leading to the formation of the 
stable Ni-PO4 complexes. The Jovanovi´c model (A.13) is based on 
similar theoretical assumptions as the Langmuir isotherm (A.10), but it 
also assumes the possibility of some mechanical contacts in the sorbent- 
sorbate system [65]. The relatively low value of the R2 (0.890) suggested 
rather practical absence of such mechanical effects in the Cs–Ni and 
phosphate anions system.

Considering that the sorption character in this new system was best 
reflected by the Freundlich (A.11), Redlich-Peterson (A.12), Hill (A.14), 
Sips (A.15), Toth (A.16), and Khan (A.17) isotherms (R2 0.988–0.992), 
the possibility of independent use of many isotherms with practically the 
same R2 – but each of them based on different theoretical foundations – 
indicated the complexity of the phosphate anions sorption on the new 
studied Cs–Ni sorbent.

Each of the discussed isotherms allows for the analysis of the process 
from a different point of view, highlighting certain details and mecha-
nisms, which provide a coherent and mutually complementary picture 
of this complex separation mechanism. This was the authors’ goal - to 
show the complexity of the analyzed phenomena and the overall

statistical fit to the measurement data by various sorption isotherms. 
First of all, for the analyzed measurement data, the dominance of the 
sorption mechanism represented by the Freundlich model (A.11), i.e. 
non-ideal and reversible sorption, with the possibility of multilayer 
sorption on a heterogeneous surface, was visible. The chemisorption 
mechanism, suggested by the isotherm model, matched very well the 
independent analytical data indicating, among others, the potential and
